speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 7 of the interview with Elizabeth Etinger on October the 11th, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: We finished the last tape just by telling us of your arrival in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to-- to go back. While I was in Poland and while I was in army, as soon they free me, I was looking for my parents, for my father, mother, and my sisters-- my family. And of course, I didn't have reply. I was living in Poland 14 years. But all this time, I sent letters to Red Cross. I sent letters to Moscow. I sent letters everywhere you can think of. Because my father was in army, I wrote the letters to army to find my father and mother and my sister. I didn't receive no reply. In 1959, I left Poland to Australia. My whole family-- my daughter, my son, me, and my husband-- come to Australia on 31st of August, 1959. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] About-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anyway, while I come here-- when I come here, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I come here, it was a very hardship in the beginning because-- but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how hard it was, it's nothing to compare what I already went through, even in Poland. So I was happy to be in Australia. The beginning was-- that wasn't a very good year. There was depression. To get a job was very hard. So help-- we didn't have much help from nowhere. So I-- I was lucky after a certain period, to get a job with-- in a factory, a clothing factory, Mr. Zucker. He was in La Trobe Street. And I-- now, I wanted a-- a-- where to live. The first thing-- he took us from the ship-- is to the boarding house. It was upstairs, one room. We're all in one room. The kitchen was downstairs. We have to take you to boil even water or something. It was very hard. But it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I start to look for-- for some dwelling, where to live. And I went to the Jewish welfare. And they said that they have-- Mr. Zucker, you've got a house in Brighton Beach. And if you want, you go and have a look. So I went and had a look. And it was 5-- 5 pounds a week. And of course I didn't have. But he gave me a job in his factory. This why I have a job and a house to live in with my family. So it wasn't that bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but for my husband to get a job was much harder. You see, as you remember, for a woman was less wages, for a man, more wages. And it was a different story altogether. But eventually, he got a job. And I got a job. And we were happy. We never complained. Plan We had a roof under us. We could have paid us rent. Went-- children went to school. And we went-- we are out of [LAUGHS] Poland. And whatever my-- I wanted, I got it. I got the children in school. And they can chose what they wanted to be where they wanted to be. And I thought straight away, the best country-- the best country is Australia. First of all, there's no war. The people won't know this. And I was happy. And I'm still happy. I've lost my husband. I could have helped him, but he didn't want to have operation. He had angina. And everybody wanted to persuade-- no doctor, not his best friends couldn't persuade him to that. But, in the meantime, I want to tell. In 1961, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I was living in Brighton Beach, where-- I was working on La Trobe Street. So I used to come to Brighton by train. My children, they used to-- after 3:00, they used to be at home. So they walked to the station always to meet me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one day, I come from-- from train. And my children stay and my daughter is screaming, Mom, you've got a very important letter! And I look at her. What important letter? She said, Mom, very important. So she gave me. And she opened-- but in the letter, there was a telegram with a Red Cross. And when I start to read, there was written, Let me know your name, on this address, David Beri. And ISO the name of my father. Can you imagine? I mean, I nearly fell down. My children have to hold me. And because it was-- I always take them to the meal bar to have a-- buy them chocolate. So this chap, he saw me. He went run-- for what? He gave me the water. And I then start to scream. I drink a bit of water. He gave them chocolate. And I took them in hand and ran all divided the house cleaning

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I don't-- still don't know what is in the letter. But I got the telegram with his-- my father's name. So I know my father is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I come home, and I opened the letter. And that letter was from my best friend in Poland. She is now in Sweden, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sabina [INAUDIBLE]. In this letter, she tells-- tells me that one day-- the-- the house that-- the flat that we lived, we sold to one person we knew. And my friend was living third floor. I was leaving first floor. So to this chap came a telegram from my father. And when they come and said, a telegram for Liza Etinger, he said, oh, she is in Australia. So he-- I took this telegram and send it back to Russia to-- to the post office. In the meantime, he knew my friend. So at night, she-- he went to the-- Sabina. I said, Sabina, I had a telegram from Russia to Liza. So he-- she said, where is the telegram? I said that she went to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she went. So right in the morning, she went to all post offices and found this telegram. And see, she sent me this telegram and this letter. But she wrote me like this. Liza, if it's-- he asked me for your-- if it's your father, he asked me for your father. No, no, no. If it's your father, let me know because I-- I want to let him know to give you your address. In the meantime, while I have this, she received a letter from my father. that he found out that Liza is in Australia. But it didn't come to Sabina. It's come to the block, you know, in every one of flat blocks, there is a committee, you know. It comes to committee, a letter to ask, my daughter, I lost her. And I didn't see her for 25 years. Please, I know that she's in Australia. Whoever knew her or know where is she now, please let me know. And he give his address.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one went to-- [POINTS] because Sabina now knew. She was-- she was a survivor. But she was in Russia all of the time while Poland was occupied. So when she saw this-- and the-- the telegram that-- she sent me telegram. In the meantime, she-- she read this-- she-- and this, 'please, let me know.' So while she sent this telegram come to me, she sent him a letter that I think this is your daughter. And I send you a photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, oi, this has got-- when I got this letter-- this telegram, I-- of course, next morning, I went and sent a telegram that yes, I am Elizabeth. I am Liza. You are my father. And I would like to know what happening to our family, where is sister, mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- in next day, I got that all alive and please-- and we are happy to hear that you are alive. Because they never look for me. I was looking for them. And it took me so long to get them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't they look for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1969, while I was here. 14 years I was in Poland, and I couldn't find-- I don't know why. That's what happened. That's my destiny to find out about all my family in-- here, in Australia. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if he made any attempt to find you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I-- I just-- I don't know. Because apparently, they didn't found my father anyway. And They found my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because in Poland, when I found out that my uncle is alive, I put in a list that-- whom I want to find, the name of my uncle. It's the same Beri. But he is Esai, and my father is David, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what happened. Esai had one only child, a son, which [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he presumed died in the first days of the war. But he couldn't believe. He was very young. So he was looking for his son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when they found Beri, they didn't look at David or Esai. So they sent-- the Red Cross sent to my uncle that I am alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was short before that they-- they found me here, you know. It was in 1961, I suppose. So what my uncle did, he write to my father and told him, Liza is alive, and she's in Poland. He didn't know. Red Cross didn't know because from here, I stopped already looking at those-- when I stay a bit longer, maybe I'll start again. But I stopped looking. So then father start to-- he rang Red Cross and told them, that's not my-- my-- my brother's daughter, but mine. Give me the address of where she live. And they send him address. And that's how it started. That's how he-- I found them, you know, and he found me. But they were sure that, you know, I'm not alive. My sister gave her daughter name after me, Liza.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters didn't say whether they tried to look for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't They were sure I-- not alive. Nobody looked for me. I looked for them. But they all were alive. The only thing-- when I found that they are alive, I want to see them. And I start to-- because I couldn't go. Because I wasn't kosher with Russia-- so I couldn't go to Russia. But I was still a citizen of Poland. I got a passport and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided to write my girlfriend, Sabina, to invite my father and mother to Poland. And I will come, too. I organize here everything. So my-- my girlfriend, she straight away sent them an invitation. They went. And they said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they said that they will let him know. And then was-- probably allowed him to go. So when my father wrote me that you must certainly come to that-- Sabina already send them. And they've got it. And they decide-- they told him that I will send. So I prepare everything here-- the ticket, the plane, the-- everything to go to Poland. What year was that, '61 or '62?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I was?

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '61. It's this same year. I wanted-- as soon as possible. So-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or was it-- no, it was in '62, I suppose. I'm not sure when-- what it is-- Now I found them, '61. So-- and I've got already-- done everything-- prepared. I receive a phone call, all of them in one booth, talking to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- from Russia, from [INAUDIBLE], where they lived-- that they said, you're not allowed to go to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: They--

speaker: interviewee
text: They refused-- refusing to give them permission to go to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians. My father got sick. It was such a shock. Because my father wasn't in army then, already. He was as-- retired army officer, yes? He wasn't in the Secret Service or something that-- and he didn't see daughter for 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And communist country Poland-- who would have think? So many people from Poland went to Russia, they didn't have nobody, from Russia to Poland-- nobody. They have-- they've been allowed.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- they wouldn't let him. Of course, it's killed him. And it's killed me. I was very, very disappointed. It was the only chance to see him-- it's in Poland. Of course, since then, he got sicker and sicker. Well, I-- anyway, I lost my father and mother in one year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you did your father die?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's-- my father die in '47-- '67.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- he was in January. My mother in October, '67.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I lost them in-- within 10 months, both of them. It was a shock--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of contact--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a bitter shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: --would you have from Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wrote letters. My father sent me beautiful letters. I still keep them. It was-- he was dreaming to see me because he used to write me in letter, look, you will-- I was so busy when you were growing up that I didn't have time to spend with you to talk with you. But now, when I have time, I can't see you. You see, we were very close to father, as-- especially me. Because he was-- he didn't finish school. He was schooling himself while he was in army. He had opportunity to go to academy. And I used to go to do lessons with him, algebra and all sort of maths, and all sort of things. We were sitting at night, and I was helping him. But he was very, very intelligent person. You can talk-- oh, and what I want to mention-- he should be general, not colonel. And because of he's Jew, he was only colonel. After the war, they didn't give him the promotion that he-- they supposed to give.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My two sisters was left there when I went-- and they went to army-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one and the other. How it happened, my father went-- on '43, he got-- how you call it-- break. So he took two-- a few-- two weeks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that should-- should have. How do you call it, when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Vacation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vacation. They give him vacation because he knew that Krasnodar was-- already was taken. It was end of '43 or '44. And he went to Krasnodar to look for my parents and sisters. He found my two girls who signed papers for us. And they knew where is my sister and mother. Through my friends, who save us because of the papers that we did. And through them, he found my mother and my two sister. He went to the Nikolaevka, where they were staying. And they were there. He find them. From there, he took them to Krasnodar. And then my two sister were-- went to army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Holocaust a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember, but I don't want to think about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You can't forget. But to be sane, the less you think about it-- I-- very nervous. I was young when I went through. That's why I am still alive. If I would have be older, a lot of old people died, died. That's not a thing that you want to remember or think about it. It's not good.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that the war--

speaker: interviewee
text: But I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --experiences affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [LAUGHS], I didn't have youth, really. My best years was killed. You know how long it took me, because I was so busy-- but took me to get used to a man, to people, to-- to believe what they're saying, or to trust them if they say they this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And oh, it's-- it's a lot of things-- a lot-- it's a lot of things that stays with you, whether you want it or not because-- because what you've gone through. You see? So-- but it's not a very pleasant thing to think about. So I try to forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] And, to tell you the truth, that was a surprise-- telling the whole story because I never talk about it. Of course, my-- my-- my closest know what I went through. But I-- to tell you the truth, I want to forget. I know I won't. But if I don't think, it's-- helps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a message that you'd like to leave for your children or future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever-- I now got to-- to talk about it, I do it only for the future generation. Repute people who survived so much and went through so much, we want to leave some legacy to future generations to remember and not to forget. Because you never know when can hit you again. We're-- we are lucky that we have Israel. Because [SOBBING] as long we is-- that we-- that we have country, [CRYING] with no Israel, we're nobody. And don't-- and don't forget it. Because of Israel, they-- whether you are Jew or not, you are somebody. You've got your country. But you haven't got a country, you're nobody. And that's what they have to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't wish nobody to get through what my generation went through. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --very hard for you to tell your story today. But we really appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Yes, I would like to introduce my family, my immediate family-- my son, Mark Etinger; my daughter, Esther Fang; [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my daughter-in-law, Linda Etinger; and my three grandson-- grandchildren, Adam, Sarah, and Nathan, which I am proud; my sister, Beri, Alexsandra. And I just want to tell that I'm very proud of my family. You see, I am lucky to have the family, just as I'm lucky to be alive. But you can be alive and not be able to have family. For a long time, I thought I won't have. But I am really, really lucky. I want to say that my father and my mother died natural deaths. My father had a stroke. My mother had a sickness, which she die of. My two sister still alive. So we, in Russia side, we are the luckiest family in Russia because all our immediate-- immediate family is survived, in spite of everything. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child of a survivor, I'd just like to impress on people that I'm very lucky to be here. [LAUGHS] If mom hadn't gone through the hardships and the difficulties that she had, I certainly wouldn't be here. And I'm very, very pleased to know someone like my mom. She's an incredible person with incredible stories to tell and some horrific images of what she went through. And I'm just amazed she's here with us today. And one thing that I've-- I've learned from all of this-- and I have heard little stories over the years-- is that everything that the Holocaust survivors went through must have been pretty horrific. And it definitely went on. And if anyone questions it or feels that it didn't happen, well, here is living proof. And there's plenty of it around the world still. And we really need to stop and listen to these people and take notice. Because once, you know, sort of-- we stop listening and don't realize that this went on, we're in danger of allowing something like this to happen again. So that's basically what I'd like to say. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I confer with what my sister said. It's-- it's something-- it's unbelievable for all of us to understand, I think. Because the people who haven't experienced it, it's just-- a-- it's-- it's really impossible to comprehend. I haven't really heard a lot of things from my mom because she never really sat down and spoke to me. And perhaps she was just being-- what's the word I'm looking for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Protective. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Protective of us. But I've heard bits and pieces of things that my mom went through. And I can't see-- look at my children here, and I can't see that any of my kids or myself or my family would have been able to would get through those times. It's-- there's a certain breed of people in those days that just were able to get through. And it's amazing to us all. I'm very proud of my mom to have been able to do that. And I suppose the most important thing I want to say is that no one should be allowed to forget what happened. And it's important that our kids listen and understand the things that our family went through-- our grandparents went through-- your grandparents went through, and our parents went through. And this is what it's all about, I think, the fact that we just don't forget and-- and, you know, keep it in our--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our life.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hearts and in our mind for the rest of our lives and pass it on to our children. It's the most important part of all this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would describe my father. And sitting, it's my grandmother, Shmaia. And the standing up is my oldest-- no, auntie--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: --which is-- which was in America. The year of this, I wouldn't know. That's very long. It's pre-revolution time, that much I can tell you. This is my mother when she was 18 years of age. And of course, it was pre-revolution time. That's my whole immediate family-- my father, my mother. On the left side is me. As a child, I have-- there, I was nine years old. Then, on the right side, I've got my middle sister, which about seven. And my-- and sitting on my father's lap is my youngest sister, which was four years old. That's my two sisters. On the left is Raia, the middle sister. On the right is my youngest sister, Shura. OK, this is my family after the war. As you see, I am not there. And those all photos I got in 1978, when I visit my sisters in Russia. So there goes-- my father, mother, Raia, and Shura.

speaker: interviewer
text: --You want that that's-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --still in-- yes. This picture was taken straight after the war finished. It was about, oh, I would say, June in 1954, soon when I start to organize on the time and group for army, Red Army. As you see, I-- I sit and play piano-accordion. And this is officer and soldiers from Red Army, some of them singing, some of them just listening, and some of them have a dance. And this picture is taken right after war in July, 1945, as their previous picture. As you see, I still entertaining on the piano accordion a lot of officers and soldiers. That is my-- me and my husband celebrating anniversary. And it's in Australia. Approximately it will be 1966 or '67. This is me and my husband in Israel in 1978, in Haifa. This is my father and mother. He's already retired in civil clothing. And it's approximately in 1965. Yes, that's in Israel in 1995, in September, when I visit. And that's my-- on the left side, standing up in glasses, is my cousin, Fiera, which lives now in Israel. And she is cousin-- her father and my mother was sister and brother. And this is all her family. On the right side, second, it's me. I won't tell you all of names of those. But that's grandchildren, children, and children-in-law, and all her family. Here I've got, on the ride side, my eldest grandson, which is now 22. He wasn't able to be here because he's working. And on Friday, he working light. So I would like to show him. Beside him is little Adam, which was there about seven or eight. And my eldest was then about 16 or 17. On the right side, that's my niece, my youngest sister daughter. And her name is Liza, just-- they give the name after me.
